stories.title: Nonprofit pitches housing conversion at Palo Alto site with SB 79 proposal
stories.excerpt: A small office building in Palo Alto could give way to a multifamily building not far from one of the city’s Caltrain stops.  San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project proposed the plan to erect a six-story building at 555 College Avenue with 70 apartments and roughly 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space, Mercury News reported. The nonprofit purchased the property in 2023 for $7.1 million, according to documents filed with the Santa Clara County Recorder’s Office.  The building would consist of five stories of housing above a ground floor that could accommodate retail, dining or other commercial uses. The […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Minority Television Project proposes 70-unit, 6-story project at 555 College Ave, Palo Alto using SB 79
Property purchased for $7.1M in 2023; transit-oriented development near California Ave Caltrain station
Nonprofit also pursuing 24-unit office conversion at 127 Lytton Ave, both leveraging new state density allowances
stories.summary: A San Mateo nonprofit is advancing two multifamily projects in Palo Alto totaling 94 units using California's Senate Bill 79, which streamlines approvals for transit-oriented housing within half-mile of major transit hubs. The 555 College Ave project—a 70-unit six-story building with ground-floor retail on a $7.1M acquisition—exemplifies how nonprofits and developers are capitalizing on SB 79 to build taller and denser near Caltrain. Both projects target the California Ave Caltrain station corridor.
stories.impact: SB 79 is materially accelerating entitlements and feasibility for mid-rise multifamily near Bay Area transit nodes. In Silicon Valley's supply-constrained Palo Alto submarket, this signals a pipeline shift from office redevelopment toward aggressive residential densification. As AI-driven tech hiring continues, these pre-entitled transit sites are becoming high-velocity acquisition targets for developers ahead of rental-demand spikes; developers should monitor nonprofit and public-land partnerships in similar Bay Area corridors where state law now overrides local zoning.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Arranges $14M Sale, Financing of Illinois LA Fitness-Anchored Retail Property
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap announced the sale of a LA Fitness-anchored, four-suite retail property in Mundelein, Illinois. The property sold for $13.976 millkion. Marcus & Millichap Capital Corporation, a subsidiary of Marcus & Millichap, arranged $9.78 million in acquisition financing.“The Sharko | Weisenbeck | Mendoza Group was the second broker to list this deal, speaking volumes for our proprietary database and Marcus’ platform,” said Austin Weisenbeck, senior managing director. Weisenbeck and Sean Sharko, investment specialists in Marcus & Millichap’s Chicago Oak Brook office, had the exclusive listing to market the property on behalf of the seller, an experienced real estate investor/developer in the Chicagoland area. The Sharko | Weisenbeck | Mendoza Group also procured the out-of-state buyer who was in a 1031 exchange. Dean Giannakopoulos, senior managing director, also based in the firm’s Chicago Oak Brook office, secured the financing.The 89,357-square-foot shopping center is situated on 7.48 acres at 1555 S. Lake St. Built in 2019, the property is anchored by LA Fitness, with Kids Empire, The Dog Stop and The Picklr Club among its other tenants. The post Marcus & Millichap Arranges $14M Sale, Financing of Illinois LA Fitness-Anchored Retail Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap brokered $14M sale of LA Fitness-anchored retail in Mundelein, Illinois
Deal includes $9.78M acquisition financing from Marcus & Millichap Capital Corporation
Four-suite retail portfolio; pricing and financing typical for secondary market retail anchored by fitness tenants
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap arranged a $13.976 million acquisition and $9.78 million in debt financing for a four-suite, LA Fitness-anchored retail property in Mundelein, Illinois. The brokers highlighted their proprietary database and platform strengths in executing the transaction.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. This is a secondary-market retail transaction anchored by a single service tenant. No signal on residential demand, supply, cost, or capital availability affecting apartment development.

stories.title: Colliers Facilitates Sale of 954K-SF Industrial Portfolio in Indianapolis
stories.excerpt: Colliers announced the sale of a six-building industrial portfolio totaling 954,072 square feet located throughout the Northwest, Northeast and Southwest submarkets of the Indianapolis metropolitan area. The portfolio includes properties at 7555 Woodland Drive, 7645 Woodland Drive, 5350 Lakeview Parkway and 7601 Winton Drive in Indianapolis, as well as 12001 Exit 5 Parkway in Fishers and 1380 Perry Road in Plainfield, Indiana. Colliers Industrial Capital Markets advisors Alex Cantu, Alex Davenport, Jeff Devine, Steve Disse and Tyler Ziebel represented seller EQT in partnership with local market advisors Jason Speckman, Jimmy Cohoat and Andrea Hopper. The portfolio was acquired by MLG Capital, based in Brookfield, Wisconsin. “Indianapolis continues to benefit from its position along major Midwestern transportation corridors and as a Midwestern growth market. The portfolio locations in Park 100, Fishers and Plainfield are some of the highest demand user markets in the city,” said Cantu. “We’re seeing strong interest in Midwestern markets, particularly Indianapolis, as investors continue to recognize the region’s economic momentum, strategic location, and long-term growth potential.” The post Colliers Facilitates Sale of 954K-SF Industrial Portfolio in Indianapolis appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Colliers brokered sale of 954K-SF industrial portfolio across six Indianapolis-area buildings for undisclosed price
MLG Capital acquired from EQT; assets in Park 100, Fishers, Plainfield submarkets
Investors citing Indianapolis logistics position, Midwestern growth momentum, strong user demand
stories.summary: Colliers facilitated the sale of a six-building, 954,072-square-foot industrial portfolio spanning Northwest, Northeast, and Southwest Indianapolis and outlying Fishers and Plainfield. Buyer MLG Capital, based in Wisconsin, acquired the assets as investors recognize Indianapolis's strategic location along major Midwestern transportation corridors and stable user demand.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily. However, the continued capital flow into Indianapolis logistics and industrial assets—and the stated enthusiasm for Midwestern growth markets—suggests strong investor appetite for the region's long-term fundamentals. Multifamily developers should monitor whether this industrial capital confidence translates into job growth and in-migration that strengthens residential demand in Indianapolis's expanding suburban corridors.

stories.title: Stand-Alone Data Centers Banned in Tacoma
stories.excerpt: A new interpretation of Tacoma’s zoning code clarifies that stand-alone data centers cannot be permitted under current regulations. In a joint statement released this week, Tacoma City Councilmember Latasha Palmer and Olgy Diaz said local communities have raised concerns about the rapid development of stand-alone data centers, citing potential impacts and increased utility costs. “Many communities across the country are expressing concern about the development of new independent data centers – and some are outright rejecting them – due to the real and potential impacts on amenities, local infrastructure, neighborhood noise levels, jobs, and many other issues,” said the council members in a statement. “We hear these same concerns echoed by Tacoma residents as well.” In light of these concerns, the city’s planning and development services department issued a new interpretation pursuant to the Tacoma Municipal Code, clarifying that single data centers are not permitted under Tacoma’s existing zoning code. The post Stand-Alone Data Centers Banned in Tacoma appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Tacoma city planning department clarified stand-alone data centers not permitted under existing zoning code
Council members citing resident concerns over utility costs, infrastructure strain, noise, job quality impacts
Zoning interpretation effective immediately; preempts prior permitting ambiguity
stories.summary: Tacoma's city planning department issued a new zoning interpretation clarifying that stand-alone data centers cannot be permitted under current municipal code. Council members Latasha Palmer and Olgy Diaz cited resident concerns over rapid development, utility costs, infrastructure impacts, and job quality, noting similar pushback in cities nationwide.
stories.impact: Regulatory headwind for any data-center-dependent industrial-to-residential conversion strategies in Tacoma. More important: the residential backlash against large industrial/infrastructure facilities signals potential demand for housing alternatives and community amenities in redeveloping industrial waterfronts. Multifamily developers eyeing Tacoma's older industrial or maritime zones should anticipate heightened community scrutiny of non-residential reuse and potentially stronger political/market support for residential infill, but also stricter permitting timelines.

stories.title: JLL Completes HQ Lease for Sandbox at Richmond’s Davenport Building
stories.excerpt: JLL completed a 6,576-square-foot lease in the Shockoe Slip district of Richmond, Virginia. Sandbox, a Richmond-based consulting firm and provider of fractional finance and accounting, HR, business operations and recruiting services, will occupy the ground-floor space at 1401 E. Cary Street as its new headquarters this month. Originally constructed in 1870, 1401 E. Cary Street occupies a prominent corner at Cary and South 14th streets in Richmond’s historic Shockoe Slip district. JLL’s Kaylin Farruggio and Charlie Polk represented Sandbox in the transaction. Farruggio and Gareth Jones, in partnership with John Jay Schwartz of Have Site Will Travel LTD, represented SNP Properties. JLL’s Katie Chalmers, vice president, PDS, assisted with the buildout; RCS Commercial Moving & Warehousing handled moving logistics for Sandbox. “Companies continue to prioritize workplaces that support culture, collaboration and employee experience,” said Gareth Jones, senior managing director, JLL. “1401 E. Cary Street offered Sandbox a rare opportunity to secure a highly visible headquarters location with authentic historic character and a turnkey creative office environment.” The post JLL Completes HQ Lease for Sandbox at Richmond’s Davenport Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Sandbox (Richmond consulting firm) leased 6,576 SF ground-floor HQ at 1401 E. Cary St, Shockoe Slip
Historic 1870 building; JLL brokered lease; move reflects corporate demand for distinctive office culture/experience
No financial terms disclosed; positioning emphasizes historic character and employee experience
stories.summary: JLL completed a ground-floor lease for Sandbox, a Richmond-based fractional finance and HR services firm, to occupy 6,576 square feet at 1401 E. Cary Street in Richmond's historic Shockoe Slip district. The 1870 structure, located at a prominent corner, offers turnkey creative office space and serves as Sandbox's new headquarters.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily. However, the high-profile corporate lease in a historic mixed-use district reflects broader corporate flight from suburban office parks to walkable, mixed-use urban neighborhoods. This signals demand for residential units nearby in Shockoe Slip; multifamily developers should target infill and conversion projects near such office anchors where employee density is rising and urban amenities drive demand.

stories.title: Steel IQ’s 3D-printed steel could slash builds from months to weeks
stories.excerpt: The new Lake Dallas startup Steel IQ believes that its 3D digital extrusion and printing is the sustainable future for affordable housing in America.  One of the major problems in the United States’ development pipeline is a lack of skilled construction workers. Steel IQ’s 3D printed steel frames may cost more to install, but require fewer skilled workers and cut down on framing timelines. The process uses a  digital model of a building, finds its dimensions with AI assistance and prints it in steel, down to the holes for the screws, according to the Dallas Morning News. Steel IQ’s founder […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Steel IQ (Lake Dallas startup) developing 3D-printed steel framing that reduces construction timeline from months to weeks
Technology requires fewer skilled workers, cuts labor needs and waste vs. wood framing despite higher material cost
First deployed on luxury mansion ($15M Infinity project in Frisco); affordable housing applications pending
stories.summary: Steel IQ, a Lake Dallas startup, is commercializing 3D digital extrusion and printing of steel frames that significantly accelerate construction timelines and reduce skilled-labor dependency. The process uses AI to digitally model buildings, prints steel frames with screw holes pre-cut, and enables on-site assembly. While steel costs more than wood, time and labor savings offset the premium. The technology was first deployed on a $15 million luxury home in Frisco.
stories.impact: Potential game-changer for multifamily construction cost and timeline in labor-constrained markets like Dallas. Reduced framing duration and lower skilled-labor dependency could improve project underwriting margins and accelerate lease-up windows, especially for affordable housing. However, adoption is pre-commercial; developers should track pilot performance on mid-rise and garden-style projects to assess ROI and insurability. Texas markets with acute labor shortages (Austin, Houston, Dallas) are logical early adopters.

stories.title: Aspen Hospitality Plans Rockefeller Center’s First and Only Hotel
stories.excerpt: Aspen Hospitality, a division of Aspen One, said it will build the first and only hotel in Rockefeller Center as part of its launch of Nell Hotels, a new luxury brand. The Nell New York is set to open in fall 2027 and will be New York City’s only Relais & Chateaux property, Representing an investment of more than $350 million, the Nell New York will be built within the existing 10 Rockefeller Plaza. The 134-key hotel will repurpose former space. “We are honored to welcome The Nell New York to Rockefeller Center and to partner with a brand that shares our commitment to creating meaningful experiences through art, dining, culture, and community,” said EB Kelly, head of Rockefeller Center and senior managing director, Tishman Speyer. “Built nearly a century ago as a city within a city, Rockefeller Center has long offered a singular destination experience. With the arrival of The Nell New York, that experience is now complete.” The post Aspen Hospitality Plans Rockefeller Center’s First and Only Hotel appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Aspen Hospitality (Aspen One) building first Nell Hotels luxury brand at 10 Rockefeller Plaza; 134-key hotel
Investment exceeds $350M; opening fall 2027; repurposing existing space within historic Rockefeller Center
Nell New York to be NYC's only Relais & Chateaux property; emphasizes culture, dining, art, community
stories.summary: Aspen Hospitality, a division of Aspen One, announced The Nell New York, a 134-key luxury hotel within 10 Rockefeller Plaza, representing over $350 million in investment and opening in fall 2027. The property will be Nell Hotels' flagship and NYC's only Relais & Chateaux property, repurposing existing space while preserving historic character.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. High-end hospitality in a premier Manhattan landmark does not affect apartment supply, demand, financing, or construction economics.

stories.title: There are no offices being built in Philadelphia
stories.excerpt: The drip of office developments springing up in Philadelphia has stilled, at least for now. Following the completion of a 438,000-square-foot building at 2000 Arch Street in the second quarter, there are no office projects in the city’s active pipeline, Bisnow reported. Chubb’s built-to-suit development was the only one still being developed, according to JLL. The lack of projects going up in the City of Brotherly Love stems in part from a dearth of tenants willing to anchor a space years before it’s actually available. “That’s been a really big hurdle when it comes to attracting out-of-market tenants,” JLL Philadelphia […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Philadelphia office pipeline zeroed out after 438K-SF Chubb project delivery in Q2 2024
Vacancy 19.8% in CBD; 492K SF absorbed (mostly Chubb); lack of pre-leased tenants stalling new speculative starts
Sublease inventory down 1M SF YoY; office inventory fell 2M+ SF; conversions scarce in Center City
stories.summary: Following delivery of a 438,000-square-foot office building at 2000 Arch Street in Q2, Philadelphia has zero office projects in active development pipeline, according to JLL. The persistent lack of pre-leased anchors is deterring speculative development; the market's mixed fundamentals are skewed by Chubb's big move. Q2 showed 19.8% CBD vacancy, 492,000 square feet of absorption (largely Chubb), but sublease inventory dropped 1 million square feet YoY.
stories.impact: Strong signal for office-to-residential conversion opportunity in Philadelphia's CBD and suburbs. With office pipeline dead and sublease supply contracting, developers should prioritize conversion entitlements and value-add deals converting Class B/C office to multifamily. However, the absence of announced conversions in Center City suggests regulatory or market headwinds; clarify zoning, parking, and adaptive-reuse incentives before land acquisition. The suburban slowdown (43% YoY leasing decline) also signals weaker office-adjacent residential demand in outlying markets.

stories.title: In Likely a Culver City Record, Black Equities Buys Multifamily Asset for $106M
stories.excerpt: Culver City, CA – February 12: An aerial of the Kirk Douglas Theatre on in Culver City. photo: Kayla Bartkowski / Los Angeles Times Stanley Black’s Beverly Hills-based investment firm is closing on a nine-figure mixed-use acquisition in another small city a few miles away. Black Equities has paid $106 million for a 115-unit property in what is said to be perhaps the largest multifamily deal in the history of Culver City, Calif., which is one of Los Angeles County’s priciest rental markets. Greystar sold the decade-old property called Access Culver City — which includes 31,000 square feet of fully leased ground-floor retail — at 8770 Washington Boulevard. Approximately $81 million of the purchase price was allocated to the residential component and $25 million to the retail space, according to Sandy Albert, managing partner at investment and development firm CityPads. The deal is roughly equal to an incredible $704,000 per residential unit and $805 per square foot for the retail component. The acquisition comes as Culver City has become one of Southern California’s hottest multifamily investment markets, fueled by media and technology employers and pro-development housing policies. The city has more than 4,500 housing units in the pipeline and has emerged as one of the few municipalities in L.A. County attracting multifamily investment. Newmark represented Greystar on the sale. Neither the buyer nor the seller immediately returned requests for comment. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Black Equities paid $106M for 115-unit mixed-use property in Culver City, CA (Access Culver City)
Deal allocates ~$81M to residential, $25M to 31K SF retail; ~$704K per unit, $805/SF blended
Greystar sold 10-year-old property; likely record for single multifamily transaction in Culver City submarket
stories.summary: Stanley Black's Beverly Hills investment firm, Black Equities, closed a $106 million acquisition of Access Culver City, a 115-unit mixed-use property with 31,000 square feet of fully leased ground-floor retail at 8770 Washington Boulevard. The deal allocated approximately $81 million to residential and $25 million to retail, representing roughly $704,000 per unit and is said to be the largest multifamily transaction in Culver City history.
stories.impact: High-priced land sale in one of LA County's costliest rental submarkets signals strong institutional investor appetite for Culver City and the Westside despite tight acquisition yield. The $704K/unit pricing reflects deep value-add or value preservation positioning rather than new development; however, the large capital deployment suggests confidence in long-term rent growth and low vacancy. Multifamily developers should monitor nearby entitlement and land-bank activity; Culver City's limited supply and no-rezone pressure make infill sites scarce and expensive, favoring conversion and value-add over ground-up development.

stories.title: Palm Beach County Pauses Data Center Applications
stories.excerpt: South Florida has entered the data center debate. In one of the first moves to regulate the construction of the asset class in the region, the Palm Beach County Commission paused data center proposals this week until it adopts appropriate zoning, following pushback from residents. Commissioners this week voted 5-2 to freeze new data center applications targeting unincorporated parts of Palm Beach County under a zoning-in-progress doctrine, which allows local governments to suspend proposals while they evaluate changes to zoning regulations. In another vote, commissioners directed county staff to draft a moratorium on new data center proposals. They are expected to finalize the ordinance next month, after which the moratorium would remain in effect for up to a year, or until new zoning regulations are adopted. “This temporary moratorium will allow sufficient time to evaluate the potential impact of these facilities,” said Palm Beach County Mayor Sara Baxter, who initiated the moratorium. “Palm Beach County must ensure that development at this scale is considered with full transparency and a clear understanding of long-term impacts on infrastructure, resources and residents.” The suspension came as concerns grew over one application, which is scheduled to go before the commission next week. Despite the votes, the development, called Central Park Commerce Center, will be allowed to undergo a review since it was filed before the moratorium. The development, also known as Project Tango, called for the construction of a 3.7 million-square-foot data center across 202 acres at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard in Loxahatchee, facing Route 98, near the Arden residential development and Saddle View Elementary School. It’s a joint venture involving Atlanta-based developer TPA Group, infrastructure contractor Phillips, the Milstein family’s New York-based Ogden Cap Properties and Loxahatchee Groves-based Palm Beach Aggregates. Despite the rapid rise of artificial intellige
bullets: Palm Beach County Commission voted 5-2 to pause data center applications under zoning-in-progress doctrine
Pause effective immediately pending zoning evaluation; formal moratorium vote expected next month lasting up to 1 year
Residents objected to scale, transparency, and impacts; first major regulatory action on data centers in South Florida
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners voted to freeze new data center applications in unincorporated areas pending zoning revisions, following resident pushback over potential facility scale and impacts. A formal moratorium ordinance is expected next month and would remain in effect for up to one year or until new zoning rules are adopted. County officials cite the need to evaluate impacts on infrastructure, services, and neighborhood character.
stories.impact: Similar to Tacoma: regulatory headwind for industrial/data-center uses in South Florida. Multifamily implications are mixed. If data-center moratorium signals broader resident opposition to large industrial redevelopment, it may block conversion of aging industrial/logistics land to residential. Conversely, if it redirects capital away from speculative infrastructure toward residential infill and mixed-use, it could strengthen rents in established residential corridors. Monitor whether Palm Beach County's formal moratorium (next month) includes carve-outs for mixed-use development or adaptive reuse; that will clarify risk for any conversion plays.

stories.title: SF approves another condo project for fast-densifying Market Street corner
stories.excerpt: San Francisco officials gave final approval to a condominium project planned for a long-entitled Market Street development site.  Property owner Keller Grover secured the city’s approval for a 23-story, 201-unit condominium tower on the site of a historic Mission Revival building at 1965 Market Street, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The entitled property is now expected to be listed for sale, with brokers betting it could establish a new benchmark for development sites approved under California’s expanded housing laws. “It will be a barometer of what is going on because it’s the first project going on the market that was […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: San Francisco approved 23-story, 201-unit condo tower at 1965 Market St, designed by Keller Grover
Project evolved from 96-unit, 7-story proposal (2015) using successive state density bonus laws and SB 423
Includes 28 affordable units (28 percent) for <50% AMI; preserves historic 1870s mortuary as lobby/amenities
stories.summary: San Francisco officials approved a 23-story, 201-unit condominium tower at 1965 Market Street, representing a significant densification from a 96-unit, seven-story proposal filed a decade earlier. The project leveraged successive California state housing laws—density bonuses and SB 423 (streamlined approval)—to maximize height and units. It includes 28 affordable units for households earning less than 50 percent of AMI and preserves the historic building as the lobby and amenities core.
stories.impact: Critical signal for development-site valuation under expanded California housing law. This is the first fully-entitled Market Street site hitting the sale market under SB 423 and stacked density bonuses; brokers expect it to establish a new price benchmark for entitled sites in SF. For developers: approved-and-listed sites in tight coastal markets are now commanding premium land valuations as buyers factor in state-law certainty and expedited timelines. However, affordability requirements (28 percent AMI) compress margins; model deal economics carefully and confirm financing availability for mixed-income projects.

stories.title: Related Ross appointment fires up West Palm Beach development debate
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach residents are pushing back after the city commission appointed a Related Ross executive to the Downtown Development Authority. The appointment of Jordan Rathlev, an executive vice president at Related Ross, added fuel to an ongoing debate over downtown development, as residents demand greater transparency and a larger role in shaping the city’s future, WPTV reported.  In two public meetings this week, residents in “Save West Palm Beach” shirts expressed their concern about rapid development and high-rise construction, arguing the city is moving too quickly and prioritizing developer alignment over community interests.  They criticized what they see as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach city commission appointed Related Ross executive Jordan Rathlev to Downtown Development Authority (DDA)
Residents in 'Save West Palm Beach' campaign protested rapid high-rise growth and perceived developer favoritism
Related Ross (Steve Ross) investing $10B in city including 6M SF office, 1.4M SF condos; political backlash imminent
stories.summary: West Palm Beach residents pushed back after the city commission appointed Jordan Rathlev, an executive vice president at Related Ross, to the Downtown Development Authority, which oversees public safety, economic development, and downtown character. Residents cited concern about rapid high-rise development and city prioritization of developer interests over community input. Related Ross, led by billionaire Steve Ross, is investing an estimated $10 billion in West Palm Beach projects, including 6 million square feet of office and 1.4 million square feet of condos.
stories.impact: Community resistance to density and developer concentration in West Palm Beach is hardening. While Related Ross's massive pipeline signals capital confidence and rental/condo demand, rising NIMBY opposition and political risk may slow approvals, increase concession requirements (public realm, affordability, community benefits), and lengthen entitlement timelines. Developers should expect heightened public scrutiny, demand for transparency, and possible moratorium or design/density caps. The city's pause on its downtown master plan signals policy uncertainty; coordinate early with city leadership and resident groups to minimize approval friction.

stories.title: Douglaston Realty Offloads 7 Cornelia Street in West Village for $25M
stories.excerpt: A business partner involved with the owner of the SoJo Spa Club in New Jersey signed mortgage documents tied to the sale of a West Village building, according to property records. While it’s unclear if Eun Rae Jo is involved in the $25 million purchase of 7 Cornelia Street, the involvement of Hyun Jun An, an investor and partner working alongside Jo, on the financing side raises the question of whether the famed spa owner has acquired yet another Manhattan property. The buyer in the deed transfer filed to property records on Thursday afternoon was masked behind a shell corporation lacking any indication of the beneficial owner. An was the authorized signatory on the $17.6 million mortgage documents from Woori America bank, according to property records. Douglaston Realty Management was the seller, alongside other entities that had a stake in the 2013 acquisition of the property for $24.6 million. Douglaston did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Neither Jo nor An could be reached for comment. Both have been working together on deals recently, including the acquisition of 893 Broadway in the Flatiron District for $31 million in early June and 16 East 18th Street from Revere Capital for $16.3 million the week prior. 7 Cornelia Street sits just south of West Fourth Street, about a block and a half west of Washington Square Park. The five-story prewar building has 47 apartments and two retail slots on the ground floor, according to StreetEasy. Worker-owned cocktail bar Donna occupies one of the two ground-floor spaces. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Douglaston Realty sold 7 Cornelia St, West Village for $25M; buyer masked behind shell corporation
Hyun Jun An (investor/partner of SoJo Spa Club owner Eun Rae Jo) signed $17.6M mortgage from Woori America Bank
Uncertain beneficial ownership; potential additional Manhattan acquisition by Jo's investment vehicle
stories.summary: Douglaston Realty Management sold 7 Cornelia Street in Manhattan's West Village for $25 million. The buyer used a shell corporation, obscuring the beneficial owner, though property records show Hyun Jun An—an investor and partner working with SoJo Spa Club owner Eun Rae Jo—as authorized signatory on a $17.6 million mortgage from Woori America Bank.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. This is a single small West Village building sale with opaque ownership; no signal on residential supply, demand, financing, or development pipeline.

stories.title: Siemens Financial, First Horizon Lend $65M on Palm Beach Gardens Medical Complex
stories.excerpt: Siemens Financial and First Horizon have supplied $64.8 million of construction financing to develop a hospital and medical outpatient complex in Florida’s Palm Beach County, Commercial Observer has learned. Sina Companies secured the loan for the planned Health Park at Avenir project in Palm Beach Gardens, Fla., that is fully leased to Jupiter Medical Center. The Real Deal reported news of the loan on Thursday but did not identify the lender. Aztec Group negotiated the debt with a team led by Ezra Katz and Sean Harrington. Located at 12001 Northlake Boulevard, Health Park at Avenir will comprise a two-story neighborhood hospital and three-story medical outpatient building spanning 101,517 square feet. The project, which will be built within the 4,752-acre Avenir in Palm Beach Gardens master-planned community, includes an approved future phase for the development of 24,000 square feet of additional medical outpatient space, according to Sina. “We are excited to welcome Jupiter Medical Center to Avenir and further expand access to high-quality health care in Palm Beach Gardens,” Malcolm Sina, executive chairman of Sina Companies, said in a statement. Siemens Financial and First Horizon did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Siemens Financial and First Horizon provided $64.8M construction financing for Health Park at Avenir medical complex
Project: 101.5K SF, two-story hospital + three-story outpatient building, 100% pre-leased to Jupiter Medical Center
Located within 4,752-acre Avenir master-planned community in Palm Beach Gardens; future phase 24K SF approved
stories.summary: Siemens Financial and First Horizon supplied $64.8 million in construction financing for Health Park at Avenir, a 101,517-square-foot medical campus in Palm Beach Gardens, Florida, fully leased to Jupiter Medical Center. The project includes a two-story neighborhood hospital and three-story medical outpatient building within the 4,752-acre Avenir master-planned community, with an approved future phase for 24,000 additional square feet.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. Medical facility construction financing does not affect residential supply, demand, or capital availability. However, the fact that Avenir is a large master-planned community is relevant context: if Avenir includes mixed-use residential, this medical anchor adds to the planned community's employment and amenity density, potentially supporting nearby multifamily absorption.

stories.title: Handshake deal could turn 17 acres of former Shell campus into Houston concert venue
stories.excerpt: The planned effort to transform a former Shell research facility into an outdoor music venue appears ready to become reality.  While the deal is not closed, a handshake deal to purchase the land has been made, according to the Houston Business Journal. Officials representing Southwest Houston’s Tax Increment Reinvestment Zone No. 20 told the outlet that the deal isn’t finalized and still needs to go before the Houston City Council, but there are agreements to purchase the 17 acres earmarked for the project.  Naina Magon, the managing principal of Hawes Hill & Associates, told the outlet that the City Council […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston handshake deal to convert 17 acres of former Shell research campus into Levitt Pavilion outdoor music venue
Agreed purchase price $5.4M (City paid $3.4M in 2019); TIRZ No. 20 financing via TIF increment
Venue part of Southwest Houston revitalization; free concerts, no designer selected yet; Houston City Council approval pending
stories.summary: A handshake agreement has been reached to purchase 17 acres of a former Shell research facility in Southwest Houston for conversion into the Levitt Pavilion Houston, an outdoor music venue operated by the Levitt Family Foundation. The agreed purchase price is $5.4 million (the city acquired the land for $3.4 million in 2019), and the project is expected to go before Houston City Council in coming months for final approval.
stories.impact: Signal of municipal and philanthropic investment in Southwest Houston revitalization. The free public-venue model and TIRZ-funded acquisition suggest city is prioritizing place-making and amenity-driven redevelopment rather than market-rate commercial real estate. For multifamily developers: this is a soft anchor that improves neighborhood perception and walkability but does not directly drive jobs or population in-migration. Monitor whether the pavilion catalyzes private infill residential, retail, or mixed-use; if so, adjacent land and structures become higher-value redevelopment targets.

stories.title: Twain Capital Supplies $65M C-PACE Loan for Colorado Hotel Project
stories.excerpt: Developer Nathaniel Kelly Foote has secured $65 million of Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) debt to complete a mixed-use project with a Marriott-branded hotel in the heart of Colorado’s Rocky Mountains, Commercial Observer has learned. Twain Capital Partners provided the C-PACE loan for the 144,597-square-foot Foote’s Rest development in Frisco, Colo., that will be anchored by an 80-key Marriott Tribute Hotel. The large-scale project will feature an underground 13,500-square-foot entertainment venue with bowling lanes and golf swing suites. It is slated for completion in October 2027. Andy Meyer, managing partner of Twain, said the deal was attractive from an underwriting perspective given Colorado’s strong C-PACE program that empowers property owners to fund 100 percent of sustainability upgrades coupled with solid hospitality demand in the Frisco market. He said Frsisco, which is 26 miles east of Vail and 15 minutes north of Breckenridge Ski Resort, has a lack of hotel supply. “Frisco continues to see more year-round travel,” Meyer told CO. “It was a challenging underwrite because there are no direct comps in the Frisco market, but a hotel of this size and scale with the amenities that are proposed we believe will achieve very strong occupancy.” Located at 500 Main Street, Foote’s Rest will also include a 4,000 square-foot ballroom, a 1,500 square feet of event space, a conference center, restaurants and a 10,000 square-foot rooftop bar. Colorado-based Greenwood Hospitality will serve as operator for the property. Foote did not immediately return a request for comment. The Foote’s Rest deal adds to Twain’s strong lending volume since the St. Louis-based lender launched a C-PACE business in late 2025 to complement its bridge lending platform. Meyer said the firm has executed $265 million of C-PACE loans over the past six months and has a pipeline $140 million under contract million that it expects to close by the end of 2026. Meyer, who joined Twa
bullets: Twain Capital Partners provided $65M C-PACE financing for Foote's Rest mixed-use project in Frisco, Colorado
144.6K SF development: 80-key Marriott Tribute Hotel, 13.5K SF underground entertainment (bowling, golf simulator)
Completion October 2027; Frisco benefits from 26-mile proximity to Vail, 15 minutes to Breckenridge; hotel supply shortage noted
stories.summary: Developer Nathaniel Kelly Foote secured $65 million in Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) debt from Twain Capital Partners for Foote's Rest, a 144,597-square-foot mixed-use project anchored by an 80-key Marriott Tribute Hotel in Frisco, Colorado. The development includes a 13,500-square-foot underground entertainment venue with bowling and golf swing suites, slated for completion in October 2027.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. This is a ski-resort-area hospitality-focused mixed-use project. However, the fact that Twain and other capital sources are actively deploying C-PACE for mixed-use projects in supply-constrained mountain markets suggests that alternative debt structures (property-tax-based financing) are available for developers seeking non-traditional capital. Multifamily developers in resort or secondary markets may explore C-PACE for sustainability-enhanced projects if conventional acquisition financing is tight.
